_comment: One benchmark run = one of these. make-report.py renders it into the on-brand report. Presentation-free where possible; the generator derives colors/styles from role/kind. Copy follows NOL8 brand voice: no em dashes, no exclamation marks, no emoji; Aergia is always the RE2 benchmark, never a NOL8 product. This report leads with the pre-index OPTIMIZATION use case (clean the data before it becomes embeddings); governance parity with the incumbent is the trust anchor.

headline.lead: Clean the data before it becomes embeddings,

lede: Everything you forward to an embedding model becomes vectors you store, search, and pay for. A deterministic pre-index policy removes what should not be there before it reaches the index: sensitive values, and repeated low-value filler. Themis runs it inline on FPGA. On this corpus it cut the forwarded payload 64.3%, verified byte-for-byte against an independent oracle. We ran the identical policy through Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2, as the benchmark.

stats.label: fewer tokens forwarded to the index
of per-call latency is network + TLS

benchmark.heading: One policy. Two engines. Only one stays correct.
benchmark.lede: The same literal policy runs the same 1,000-chunk corpus on both engines. It governs known values to tokens and strips the most-repeated low-value filler to nothing. Themis forwards 64.3% less and matches an independent oracle on every chunk. Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2, corrupts the strip on most of them. Do nothing is the null baseline.

approaches.headline: Everything reaches the index
approaches.desc: The full embedding bill. Every token forwarded downstream, sensitive values and repeated filler included.

approaches.headline: Google RE2, the known regex engine
approaches.desc: Our stand-up of Google RE2. It strips the filler but leaves fragments behind (default. becomes ault.), corrupting 876 of 1,000 chunks and polluting the vectors with garbage tokens.

approaches.headline: Govern and shrink in one pass
approaches.desc: 64.3% less, and correct on every chunk (verified against an independent oracle). The identical policy, inline on FPGA at hardware speed.

redaction.before: Customer review: Westbridge Merchant Services requested accelerated enablement for the Redwood Identity workflow.
redaction.note: Redaction is byte-identical across Themis and Aergia for all 1,000 records. The engines only diverge when stripping, shown in the appendix.

latency.heading: The engine is the smallest slice.
latency.lede: We isolated raw engine time by measuring the path in layers and subtracting transport. N = 100, medians, from the client box. On both engines the compute lands below the network round-trip, so it is reported as an upper bound. Cleaning runs inline with no latency budget to spend.
latency.callout: Reusing one connection instead of reconnecting per call drops Themis from 7.17 ms to 2.38 ms, a 3x cut with zero engine change. What is left is one network round-trip. The engine sits inside the measurement noise.

tableNote: Engine time is what remains after subtracting network RTT and TLS. On both engines it lands under the network round-trip, below what we can resolve from outside, so it is reported as an upper bound. The takeaway is not a precise figure. It is that the engine is not where the time goes.

meaning.heading: What it means for the data path.

takeaways.title: Everything you embed is a bill.
takeaways.body: Forwarded text becomes vectors you store, search, and pay for. Removing repeated filler before the index cut this corpus 64.3%. The saving scales with real data.

takeaways.title: Correctness is not optional at the index.
takeaways.body: A strip that leaves fragments poisons the vectors silently. Themis matched an independent oracle on all 1,000 chunks. The RE2 benchmark corrupted 876 of them.

takeaways.title: Govern and optimize in one deterministic pass.
takeaways.body: The same literal policy removes sensitive values and low-value filler together, inline, before anything reaches the model. One control, two outcomes.

takeaways.title: Your latency is a networking problem, not a compute one.
takeaways.body: The engine is sub-millisecond, so cleaning runs inline with no latency budget. About 97% of per-call time is TLS and round-trip. The win is the payload, not the clock.

method.def: 1,000-chunk synthetic pre-index corpus, run identically through every approach.

One literal policy, two jobs: govern known values to tokens (denied entities, watched customers, compromised accounts, payment cards, internal projects, blocked IPs), and strip the ten most-repeated low-value filler sentences to nothing. The same file is deployed to both engines.
Themis, the NOL8 FPGA (:443), and Aergia, our stand-up of Google RE2 (:444) as the benchmark. Same data, same policy. Do nothing forwards everything.

Each engine's output is adjudicated against the validation framework's independent Aho-Corasick matcher (leftmost-longest, non-overlapping literal replacement). Themis matched it 1000 of 1000; Aergia diverged on 876, every divergence a strip.
Measured as tokens forwarded versus Do nothing. Themis forwards 15,343 of 43,005 tokens, a 64.3% cut. Redaction is length-neutral; the volume cut is the filler strip.
Corpus-derived: the most-repeated exact sentences, which carry no per-chunk retrieval value. A real deployment supplies its own list; the mechanism is identical.
Decomposed from the field client box, N = 100 per layer, medians. Engine time bounded as (warm minus network RTT), an upper bound; no server-side timing hook is exposed.

methodNote: Not a concurrency or throughput saturation test. Themis is a literal matcher, so this strips exact repeated sentences; variable boilerplate is pattern work, out of scope for listMatch.

raw.heading: Show your work
raw.intro: Every number here comes from the same 1,000-chunk run. Themis output is verified byte-for-byte against an independent oracle, so these are receipts, not estimates.

breakdown.title: Where the 64.3% comes from
rows: known values become tokens, safety not size
breakdown.verified: Verified byte-for-byte against an independent oracle: 1000 of 1000 chunks match.

stripRules.title: What we removed, and why
stripRules.note: The ten most-repeated exact sentences in the corpus. Each is generic framing that adds no per-chunk retrieval value, so it is stripped everywhere it appears. Counts are occurrences across the 1,000 chunks.
rows: Governance teams want deterministic pre-index controls so sensitive text does not land in embeddings by default.
The analyst assistant should prioritize incident timelines, account context, and remediation notes over boilerplate.
The AI search workflow is only useful if customer-risk context is preserved while low-value noise is suppressed early.
Security operations wants embedding spend focused on high-value investigative content rather than repetitive status traffic.
Retrieval quality improves when onboarding summaries and fraud-review notes are normalized before indexing.
Customer operations added a short note explaining that the account requires special handling during after-hours support.
Tier-2 support escalated the case after the customer reported repeated sign-in failures from a new device profile.
The payments team attached an investigation summary covering disputed transactions, refund notes, and fraud indicators.
The fraud queue contains a mix of confirmed abuse, false positives, and cases awaiting additional context.
A compliance analyst documented the onboarding review and recommended manual verification before account activation.

samples.title: Before and after, real corpus chunks
samples.note: Original chunk in, forwarded payload out. Governed values become tokens; stripped filler is gone. The RE2 benchmark column shows where Aergia leaves fragments behind (default. becomes ault.), the corruption that pollutes the vectors.

aggregate.note: The measured outcome is the data forwarded downstream, the payload that becomes embeddings. Reduction is versus Do nothing. Aergia forwards more than Themis, and its payload includes corruption fragments rather than clean signal. Process metrics (CPU, memory) are omitted on purpose: they measure the test harness, not the customer outcome. From a single clean run of the optimization policy on both live engines.

footer.tagline: The AI data plane. Hardware-class, inline, wire-speed. Clean the data before it becomes cost.
